key_insight: Pareto fronts in topology optimization can be disconnected and nonconvex because they are composed of local front segments, and scalarization choice strongly affects how well the front is sampled.
engineering_relevance: This matters for engineers balancing competing design goals such as compliance, volume, stress, and dynamic performance, because it changes how reliably trade-offs are discovered and compared.
method: Compared weighted-sum, ε-constraint, and Pascoletti-Serafini scalarizations.
Analyzed theoretical properties of the scalarization methods for multiobjective topology optimization.
Ran multiple bi-objective case studies spanning volume, compliance, maximum stress, and dynamic compliance.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a theoretical comparison plus a numerical study across many bi-objective examples, showing more uniform Pareto approximations with Pascoletti-Serafini and clustering with weighted-sum.
watch_item: Weighted-sum can miss nonconvex regions of the Pareto front, so relying on it alone may give an incomplete view of feasible trade-offs.
paper_title: Novel insights into Pareto fronts in multiobjective topology optimization and a comparative study of scalarization strategies
